coredata.dc:description: Carbon Nanodots (CNDs) generated from biomass wastes have a wide range of biological functionalities and are used in a variety of applications. Especially, the biomedical applications shown by the CNDs derived from biomass waste materials received great attention from researchers around the globe. Herein we report the synthesis of CNDs from rice husk biomass waste using a hydrothermal method. XRD pattern demonstrates the amorphous nature of CNDs with hexagonal graphite structure. Microscopic images confirmed that as-synthesized CNDs are in spherical morphology with an average size of 25–30 nm and average interplanar spacing of 0.265 nm. XPS, Raman, and FTIR spectral analyses were performed to examine the presence of functional groups and defects in CNDs. Excitation-dependent emission properties of CNDs (ФF 9.6%) were studied by UV–Vis., Contour, and fluorescence techniques. In response to the findings of the cytotoxicity experiment and the CNDs' strong emission, we carried out several biostudies to prove its importance. The CNDs possess outstanding cell viability (98%), excellent anti-inflammatory activity of 33% of LOX, 34% of COX, 45% of NO inhibition at 100 μL, significant protein denaturation (62%; IC50 20.04 μL/mL), and bright bioimaging on Zebrafish characteristics, which accentuate the perception of waste to wealth.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CNDs. (a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman spectra, (c) XPS spectrum, (survey (c1), C 1s (c2), O 1s (c3), and N 1s (c4)), and (d) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of CNDs (slit width 5 nm) (inset: CNDs solution under daylight and UV light).
(a) Contour mapping of CNDs with λ ex 370 nm and λ em 456 nm, (b) 3D Fluorescence emission spectra of CNDs (λ ex 400–550 nm).
FESEM images of CNDs (a→c in 1 μm, 200 nm, and 100 nm scale, respectively); Elemental mapping from EDAX analysis of CNDs (d→f as carbon, oxygen, and respectively).
HRTEM images of CNDs (a→e in 100, 50, 20, 5, and 2 nm scale, respectively); (f) SAED pattern, and (inset c) histogram.
Cell viability results for the evaluation of cytotoxicity for CNDs with L929 cells. The asterisks indicate significant differences (**p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001) for control and CNDs, respectively.
Anti-inflammatory activity of CNDs using MPO inflammatory enzyme inhibition. The asterisks indicate significant differences (*p < 0.02) for LPS and CNDs, respectively.

Graphical representation of the anti-inflammatory activity of CNDs; (a) LOX, (b) COX inflammatory enzyme inhibition. The asterisks indicate significant differences; **p < 0.02, ***p < 0.001, for LPS and CNDs, respectively.

Graphical representation of the anti-inflammatory activity of CNDs; (a) Inducible Nitric Oxide assay, (b) Estimation of Cellular Nitrite Levels. The asterisks indicate significant differences; (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001) for LPS and CNDs, respectively.
Illustration of anti-inflammation activity of CNDs by inhibition of protein denaturation method. The asterisks indicate a significant difference (**p < 0.01) for CNDs.

Fluorescence imaging of zebrafish larvae's morphological evaluation at varying concentrations of CNDs. The fluorescence emission was obtained as blue emission. (Left → Right) Fluorescence bright field, DAPI staining, and Merged images. The scale bar for all the images is 400 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic representation for the synthesis of CNDs.

head.ce:title: Multipurpose biological applications of excitation-dependent fluorescent carbon nano dots emanated from biomass waste

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Nanodots (CNDs) generated from biomass wastes have a wide range of biological functionalities and are used in a variety of applications. Especially, the biomedical applications shown by the CNDs derived from biomass waste materials received great attention from researchers around the globe. Herein we report the synthesis of CNDs from rice husk biomass waste using a hydrothermal method. XRD pattern demonstrates the amorphous nature of CNDs with hexagonal graphite structure. Microscopic images confirmed that as-synthesized CNDs are in spherical morphology with an average size of 25–30 nm and average interplanar spacing of 0.265 nm. XPS, Raman, and FTIR spectral analyses were performed to examine the presence of functional groups and defects in CNDs. Excitation-dependent emission properties of CNDs (ФF 9.6%) were studied by UV–Vis., Contour, and fluorescence techniques. In response to the findings of the cytotoxicity experiment and the CNDs' strong emission, we carried out several biostudies to prove its importance. The CNDs possess outstanding cell viability (98%), excellent anti-inflammatory activity of 33% of LOX, 34% of COX, 45% of NO inhibition at 100 μL, significant protein denaturation (62%; IC50 20.04 μL/mL), and bright bioimaging on Zebrafish characteristics, which accentuate the perception of waste to wealth.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Considerable interest has been paid to the use of nanosized light-emitting materials with diverse optical and electrical properties in a wide range of applications, including lights, power displays, and other biological and environmental devices, as well as solar cells [1]. Among nanomaterials that emit light, such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), lanthanides, polymer dots, dye-doped nanoparticles, carbon-based dots (carbon quantum dots (CQDs), Carbon Nano Dots (CNDs), and carbon-dots (CDs)) have emerged with great promise for biological applications [2], whereas the majority of other nanomaterials have cytotoxicity, photobleaching, poor stability, and paucity in tunability as drawbacks [3]. Furthermore, compared to conventional quantum dots, the CQDs, CNDs, and CDs possess distinctive biological features that make them ideal for bioimaging, biosensing, and drug delivery [4].

Zero-dimensional (OD) quasi-spherical nanoparticles known as CNDs have several exceptional properties, such as good luminescence characteristics, functionalizable, photostability, minimal cytotoxicity, remarkable water solubility, favorable biocompatibility, and low environmental risk [5]. Notably, CNDs are a unique tool for fluorescence labeling and imaging in the biomedical field due to permeability, weak interactions with proteins, resistance to swelling and photobleaching, ease of removal from the body, low cost, and immune system evasion in addition to the above-mentioned properties [6–8]. These aptitudes give them a strong foundation for diagnosis and various medical applications, for example, live cell imaging [9]. In cultivated tobacco cells, photosensitized-conjugated CDs were shown to function as fluorescent markers for endocytosis pathways [10]. Super-CNDs for photothermal treatment and in vivo tumor photoacoustic imaging [11]. Enhanced dark-field microscopy (EDFM) coupled with hyperspectral imaging (HSI) was used to quantify the light-scattering signals of CNDs in the cells with an emphasis on possible nuclear penetrability [12]. CDs were used as imaging agents to create various labeling distributions of hepatoma cells (HepG2) and normal human liver cells (LO2) [13].

CNDs can aid in diagnosis by providing information about the generous, size, and position of the human cancer sites. The regulated administration of drugs to the intended location of the body can benefit from CND's therapeutic effects [14]. Additionally, CND helps to measure pH, detect pH variation, and bioimaging in living organisms like zebrafish and mice [15]. Among different methods, the CNDs are produced from precursors that are either organic, inorganic, bioorganic, or biowaste. The hydrothermal treatment, ultrasonic treatment, thermal decomposition, pyrolysis, carbonization, microwave synthesis, and solvothermal procedures were applied, particularly in the bottom-up approach to generate CNDs from different precursors [16–31]. However, it is possible to create high carbon content at minimal cost by recycling biomass waste. The transformation of biomass into novel nanomaterials for a variety of applications is a promising example of a “waste to wealth” approach [32]. The main elements of biomass are hemicellulose, cellulose, and lignin. This non-toxic, useful, and sustainable material is rich in carbon and has been widely used as a feedstock for the synthesis of numerous highly valuable carbon compounds, including graphene, fullerene, carbon nano-fullerenes, and CNTs [33].

For the synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials from biomass waste, the green synthesis methods are used as energy-saving and low-cost materials which have received great attention [34]. The most prevalent agricultural biomass sources are rice residues, wheat stems, corn straws, and sugarcane bagasse [35]. Since it is regarded as agricultural waste, a renewable resource, is comparatively inexpensive, and is simple to obtain in large quantities, rice husk is a good contender as a beginning precursor [36]. Additionally, the rice husk contains inorganic components like silica, carbon, and potassium as well as organic components like lignin, hemicellulose, and cellulose that can be exploited for a variety of bio-functionalities [37]. The materials with a variety of biofunctionalities were used in widespread biological applications. The preparation of doxorubicin-entrapped CDs (DOX-CDs) is applied for better intracellular drug delivery and bioimaging [38]. Bright fluorescent CQDs coated with polyethyleneimine (PEI-CQDs) display green emission for the determination of thrombin and ATP in spiked serum samples [39]. The highly luminescent CDs synthesized by using folic acid (FA) were utilized for extraordinary targetability to cancer cells and promoted folate receptor-mediated cellular uptake successfully [40]. CDs prepared from the amino acid tyrosine inhibit fibrillation of hIAPP, reduce hIAPP-induced cell toxicity, and block membrane disruption by the peptide [41].

In the current communication, we claim that the CNDs, a wealth material, formed from waste material as a preliminary novelty. Though there are many biomass wastes are existing, the rice husk was chosen due to their higher carbon (41.92%) content and other significant components such as N2 (1.85%), Si (0.47%), cellulose (50%) (for pharmaceutical applications), and lignin (30%) (a carbon source) [42,43]. Since 24 million tons of rice husk biomass waste were produced every year in India, the rice husk (raw material) for CQD preparation is readily available. CDs exhibit excellent bio-compatibility along with photo-stability, chemical inertness, ease of surface functional group modification, bright fluorescent emission, rich hydrophilic surface, stable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, excellent water solubility, and low cost. The bright emission of CDs provides the opportunity for dynamic and real-time sensing with faster response [44]. Biomass waste is a good choice for the production of CDs owing to its abundance, wide availability, eco-friendly nature, and a source of low-cost renewable raw materials such as cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, carbohydrates, proteins, etc [45]. Also, the hydrothermal technique is an easy and fast synthesis method that produces stable, economical, and excellent quality CQDs, with high yield.

In this article, we describe potential CNDs derived from rice husk biomass waste used as a carbon source for multiple biological applications, namely anti-inflammatory, protein denaturation inhibition, and bioimaging. The hydrothermally prepared CNDs morphology, purity, structural defect, and existence of functional groups were investigated by microscopy and spectroscopy techniques. The effect of CNDs and their behavior with RAW macrophage cells for the immunological functioning upon the anti-inflammatory activity was examined. The protein denaturation inhibition activity of CNDs was studied based on its properties like biocompatibility and photostability. Excitation-dependent emitting CNDs, low toxicity, efficient adsorption, and emission reliability incited us to use them for bioimaging in live zebrafish embryos. The reproducibility of the synthesis of CNDs from different batches with similar experimental conditions and raw materials was already reported in the literature [46].

The represented rice husk (collected from rice mills, Udumalpet, Coimbatore). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CQDs were collected on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Ni-filtered Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a SHIMADZU (Miracle 10, US) spectrometer. Raman spectra were captured using a spectrometer WiTec alpha 300, Germany. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is analyzed by Physical Electronics. FESEM images were captured on SIGMA HV-carl Zeiss with Bruker quanta 200-Z10 EDS’ detector. HRTEM images were recorded on Jeol/JEM 2100, Japan. UV–Visible absorption spectrum measurements were carried out on a Shimadzu UV-1900 spectrometer. Photoluminescence analysis was carried out in Fluoromax Spectrofluorometer, Horiba, Japan. Fluorescence bioimaging experiments were recorded on the EVOS Life Technology spectrofluorophotometer. The anti-inflammatory activity and protein denaturation analyses were carried out at the Center For Research on Molecular Biology and Applied Science, Trivandrum, India.

A direct Methyl thiazolyl diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was used to assess the cytotoxicity of carbon nanodots. The fibroblast 3T3 cells were grown in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), which contains 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. The cells (1 × 105 per well) were seeded and grown in 24-well plates at 37 °C with 5% CO2. The cells were subjected to various concentrations of the sample (0, 5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μL) for 24 h to calculate the cytotoxicity dependent on sample concentration. The mitochondrial enzyme level was decreased by incorporating 5 mg/mL of MTT into the samples and incubating them for 4 h. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) of 1 mL was applied to each well after the incubation period to dissolve the formazan crystals. DMSO was used as a control and an ELISA reader was used to assess the absorbance at 570 nm. Research revealed the concentration required for a 50% inhibition (IC50). The morphology of the cells was also observed using an inverted phase-contrast microscope. With the help of the following equation, the proportion of viable cells was calculated (i).
Cell viability (%) = (Corrected abs. of sample/corrected abs. of control) × 100 (i)

RAW 264.7 cells were initially procured from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India, and maintained Dulbecco's modified Eagles medium, DMEM (Sigma Aldrich, USA). The cell line was cultured in a 25 cm2 tissue culture flask with DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS, l-glutamine, sodium bicarbonate (Merck, Germany), and an antibiotic solution containing: Penicillin (100 U/mL), Streptomycin (100 μg/mL), and Amphotericin B (2.5 μg/mL). Cultured cell lines were kept at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator (NBS Eppendorf, Germany). The cells were grown to 60% confluency followed by activation with 1 μL lipopolysaccharide (LPS: 1.0 μg/mL). The LPS-stimulated RAW cells were exposed to the different concentrations (25, 50, and 100 μg/mL) of sample solution and incubated for 24 h. The CND's sample solutions were added directly to the LPS stimulated well. After incubation, the anti-inflammatory assays were performed using the cell lysate.

COX activity was assayed by 100 μL cell lysate and was incubated with Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.0), glutathione 5.0 mM/L, and hemoglobin 5.0 mM/L for 1 min at 25 °C [47]. The reaction was initiated by the addition of arachidonic acid 200 mM/L and terminated after 20 min of incubation at 37 °C, by the addition of 200 μL of 10% trichloroacetic acid in 1.0 N HCl. After centrifugation and the addition of 200 μL of 1% thiobarbituric acid, the tubes were boiled for 20 min. After cooling, the tubes were centrifuged for 3 min. The COX activity was determined by reading absorbance at 632 nm and percentage inhibition of COX activity was determined as per the following formula (ii):
% inhibition = ((Absorbance of control-Absorbance of test)/Absorbance of control) × 100 (ii)

The determination of LOX activity was done by the reaction mixture (2 mL final volume) containing Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4), 50 μL of cell lysate, and sodium linoleate (200 μL). The LOX activity was monitored as an increase of absorbance at 234 nm (Agilent Cary 60), which reflects the formation of 5-hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acid [48].

Percentage inhibition of the enzyme was calculated as per the following formula (iii):
% inhibition = ((Absorbance of control-Absorbance of test)/Absorbance of control) × 100 (iii)

The cell lysate was homogenized in 50 mM potassium phosphate buffer and 0.57% hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (HTAB). The samples were centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 30 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant was assayed for MPO activity. MPO in the sample was activated by the addition of 50 mM phosphate buffer (pH 6.0) containing 1.67 mg/mL guaiacol and 0.0005% H2O2. The change in absorbance at 460 nm was measured. MPO activity was presented as units per mL of cell lysate. One unit of MPO activity was defined as that degrading 1.0 μM of peroxide per minute at 25 °C.
U = (ΔOD × 4 × Vt × dilution factor)/(L × £470 × Δt × Vs) (iv)
Where ΔOD = density change; Vt = total volume (mL) (1.1 mL); L = light path (1 cm); £470 = extinction coefficient for tetraguaiacol (26.6 mM−1 cm−1).

Nitric oxide synthase was determined by the method described [49]. The cell lysate was homogenized in 2 ml of HEPES buffer. The assay system contained 0.1 mL–2 μmol/L of l-Arginine, 0.1 mL - 4.0 μmol/L manganese chloride, 0.1 mL–10 mmol/L 30 μg dithiothreitol (DTT), 0.1 mL–1.0 mmol/L NADPH, 0.1 mL–4.0 μmol/L tetrahydropterin, 0.1 mL 10 μmol/L oxygenated hemoglobin and 0.1 mL cell lysate. An increase in absorbance was recorded at 401 nm and enzyme activity was determined as per the following equation:

Percentage inhibition of the enzyme was calculated as per the following formula (v):
% inhibition = ((Absorbance of control-Absorbance of test)/Absorbance of control) × 100 (v)

The level of nitrites was estimated as [50], 0.5 mL of cell lysate, and 0.1 mL of 3% sulphosalicylic acid were added and vortexed well for 30 min. The samples were then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. The protein-free supernatant was used for the estimation of nitrite levels. To 200 μL of the supernatant, 30 μL of 10% NaOH was added, followed by 300 μL of Tris-HCl buffer, and mixed well. To this, 530 μL of Griess reagent (1% sulphanilamide, 2% phosphoric acid, and 0.1% N-1-naphthyl ethylene diamine dihydrochloride) was added and incubated in the dark for 10–15 min, and the absorbance was read at 540 nm against a Griess reagent blank. Sodium nitrite solution was used as the standard. The amount of nitrite present in the samples was estimated from the standard curves obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antiarthritic activity by inhibition of protein denaturation method
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of CNDs such as 6.25, 12.5, 25.0, and 50 μg/mL from a stock solution of 1.0 mg/mL were used for in vitro antiarthritic activity by inhibition of protein denaturation study. The test control consists of bovine serum albumin (BSA; Fraction V- HiMedia) and 0.05 mL of distilled water. The test solution consists of 0.45 mL of BSA and different concentrations of CNDs. Product control consists of 0.45 mL of distilled water and different concentrations of CNDs. Diclofenac sodium was used as a standard solution. All the above-mentioned solutions were adjusted to pH 6.3 using 1.0 N HCl. The samples were incubated at 37 °C for 20 min and the temperature was increased to 57 °C for 3 min. After cooling, 2.5 mL of phosphate buffer was added to the solutions. The absorbance was measured using UV–Visible Spectrophotometer at 416 nm (Cary win60- Agilent).

The percentage inhibition of protein denaturation was calculated by the following equation (vi):
% inhibition = 100- [((optical density of test solution – optical density of product control) ÷ optical density of test control)) × 100] (vi)
IC50 value 20.047854 μg/mL (Calculated using ED50 PLUS V1.0 Software).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for zebrafish embryo development and bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: The zebrafish (AB strain) pathogen-free were cultured and reproduced on the zebrafish experimental platform. The zebrafish were reared in a fully enclosed circulatory system based on the standard zebrafish breeding protocols. To induce spawning, 6 male and 12 female zebrafish (1:2 ratio) were placed in the spawning tank. The temperature in the fish room was maintained at 28 °C with a 14 h light: 10-h dark cycle. The fertilized embryos were collected the next day morning. The fertilized embryos were transferred into a clean petri dish, to remove unfertilized and dead embryos using an inverted microscope (EVOS FLC, life technologies) and washed with an exposure medium. Further, the zebrafish were transferred into a new plate for imaging using fluorescence microscopy [51].

The usual one-way analysis of variance with significant levels was used in statistical analysis to compare the means of more than two groups. Statistic evaluation Utilizing Origin data was analyzed using ANOVA and Bonferroni's test (Origin Lab, Northampton, MA, USA).

A facile hydrothermal technique was employed to prepare CNDs using rice husk biomass waste as a precursor. Initially, rice husk was washed with double-distilled water (5 × 10 mL), and collected water portions were heated for 2 h at 80 °C to get the extract. The extract was then placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and thermally treated for 6 h at 200 °C. The resulting CNDs were collected by filtering using a 0.22 μm membrane filter as an orange-brown liquid at room temperature (Scheme 1 ). An orangish-brown liquid was obtained after the filtration process. The separated aqueous solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and thus the materials with low fluorescence were separated. In this way, CNDs with high fluorescence were obtained. The final solution was kept in the refrigerator for further analysis. The as-prepared CNDs were systematically characterized before being employed in various biological applications.

The preparation of carbon-based nanomaterials (CNDs, CNPs, CDs, and CQDs) by varying biomass, and specific synthesis techniques, with significant properties, shows tremendous scope in the areas of environmental fortification, biomedical, and biosensing applications. Predominantly, the rice husk-derived CNPs with strong blue emission [52], CQDs with broad green emission [53], and carbon microspheres (CMs) with small cellulose nanocrystals [54] show linear quenching effect, good repeatability, tunable photoluminescence, uniform and distinct size distribution and compatibility, employed in metal ion sensing, wastewater treatment, and biomedical applications. Alternate forms of rice-oriented biomasses like rice powder [55] and rice bran [56] synthesized CNDs and CNPs were applied in the field of sensors, light emitting diodes, and bioimaging with a variable and strong blue emission, and high photostability properties. Further, palmyra leaves [57] and malus floribunda [58] biomass used for the synthesis of CNDs and N-doped CNDs via the hydrothermal method for enhanced compatibility on cells, excitation-dependent emission, tunable fluorescence emission, good performances in cell labeling, virtuous water solubility/dispersibility, rich hydrophilic functionalities, and eco-friendly for biosensing, bioimaging, and act as an anticancer drug.

Apart from CNDs, CNPs, CDs, and CQDs obtained from rice husk, silica-based materials were widely prepared from rice husk and applied in numerous fields. For example, Silica nanoparticles (SiNPs) for biomedical and environmental applications [59], biogenic SiNPs as food additives and for biomedical applications [60], Si-CQDs as effective dye degradation material [61], and SiNPs in white light emitting diodes [62], were synthesized via hydrothermal and rapid microwave heating methods. The aforementioned silica materials from rice husk show high water dispersibility, biocompatibility, intracellular ROS generation, mitochondrial transmembrane potential, oxidative stress-related gene expression, biocompatible, and good photocatalytic properties.

The XRD pattern of CNDs shows intense peaks at 2θ 28.4° and 43.3° which corresponds to hkl diffraction planes of (002) and (101) with hexagonal graphite structure (Fig. 1 a). In particular, the (002) broad plane illustrates the nature of graphite and partial graphitization to generate CNDs, and the (101) plane is formed from the graphene structure with in-plane diffraction [63]. The observed XRD pattern confirms the notion that the presence of several functional groups leads to the formation of CNDs with an amorphous nature.

Raman analysis was employed to investigate the structural defects in CNDs. The Raman spectra of CNDs reveal two peaks at 1340 and 1565 cm−1 which are attributed to the sp3 defects (D-band) and sp2 carbon (G-band), respectively (Fig. 1b) [64]. The D band is assigned for dangling bonds in the carbon atoms as the termination plane of disordered graphite, whereas the G band is designated for the E2g graphite mode, which is connected with the sp2 vibrations in the carbon atoms between the hexagonal lattice [65]. Interestingly, a less intense peak observed at 1447 cm−1 suggest that sp2 bonding has longer distances to form the six-fold carbon rings that consequence the amorphous carbon structure [66]. In general, the degree of disorder in CNDs is indicated by the computed ID/IG ratio. Since the calculated ID/IG ratio (1.23) is more than 1 it implies that there are numerous defects on the CND's surface. This result supports the amorphous nature of as-prepared CNDs that is in good agreement with the XRD findings.

The XPS survey spectrum of CNDs revealed the total surface composition of elements such as C, O, and N as 70.59%, 29.41%, and <0.1% (Fig. 1c1). There are no other residual element peaks were detected which confirms the purity of rice husk biomass waste-derived CNDs. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s revealed four peaks at 284.46, 285.82, 287.29, and 288.57 eV, corresponding to the CC (sp2), C–N, O–CO and CO/CN groups, respectively (Fig. 1c2). The O1s were deconvoluted to two peaks at 531.68 and 532.96 eV indicating the presence of –C-O/-N-O and –CO/-NO functional groups (Fig. 1c3). The N1s spectrum shows a peak at 399.57 eV which is assigned to C–NH2 functional group (Fig. 1c4) [67]. These XPS results conclude that the CNDs contain multiple O and N functional groups linked with C which is responsible for their stability and fluorescence emission properties [68].

The experimentally derived quantum yield (ФF) of rice husk biomass-derived CNDs is 9.6%. The ФF calculations were performed using Quinine sulfate solution (ФF = 54% at 360 nm excitation, ƞ = 1.33) as a standard reference to calculate the relative ФF of the CNDs. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 for fluorescence analysis [SIR1]. In a comparison of other biomass-derived CNDs existing in literature, the rice husk biomass-derived CNDs shows improved ФF (Table SI6).

The UV–visible absorption spectra show three peaks at λ max 216, 256, and 284 nm as displayed in Figure SI1. The λ max 216 nm peak arises due to the π → π* transition and λ max 256 and 284 nm correspond to the n → π* transition [69,70]. Excitation-dependent emission characteristics of the CNDs were determined by photoluminescence (PL) spectra with excitation wavelength from 330 to 430 nm by the sequential increment of 10 nm (Fig. 1d). When the excitation wavelength is increased from 330 to 420 nm, the emission peak intensity of CNDs was increased with a redshift (∼47 nm) from 429 to 476 nm. In disparity, it is noticed that the fluorescence emission intensity was suddenly decreased with a negligible redshift (∼1 nm) upon exciting at 430 nm. Therefore, it is confirmed that for these rice husk biomass waste materials, the highest fluorescence emission intensity wavelength is 476 nm, and the optimum excitation wavelength is 420 nm [71]. These results show the optical behavior of CNDs as excitation-dependent emission, which is associated with the non-uniform particle size and distribution of different surface defect states close to the fermi level of CNDs [72]. Furthermore, as a surface functional group on CNDs, the CO bonds trap energy on the surface states and generate the fluorescence spectrum [73].

The synthesized CNDs exhibit the fluorescence emission characteristic relying on its excitation wavelength which is demonstrated in Fig. 2 b. The excitation-supported emission 3D fluorescence map (Fig. 2b) supports the variation in excitation wavelength from 400 to 550 nm and the accompanying emission change. The excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior is most likely due to the various sizes of the synthesized CNDs which is well supported by Figure SI1. The adjustable emission behavior is also impacted by the CNDs' functional groups present on its surface. Furthermore, there is a slight blue shift was also observed in the 3D fluorescence emission spectra suggesting that the CNDs are free from surface defects and have slower π-transition towards the domain [74]. The CNDs' visual emission distinguishment has exhibited a broad and continuous area of contour mapping as shown in Fig. 2a. The brighter area corresponds to higher intensity emission which might be the cause of lesser particle nature. In addition, the tunable spectrum ranges of mapping were attributed owing to the inhomogeneous particle size and different excitation ranges. The red color presents the highest emission intensity, followed by orange, yellow, green, and blue, and finally, black is the lowest emission intensity [75].

The FESEM images display the agglomerated spherical nanosized CNDs and also well-defined boundaries arranged in the form of a carbon chain (Fig. 3 a–c). Notably, the particles like the carbon chain of nanospheres were interconnected by weak van der Waals force [76]. The elemental distribution in CNDs was made by mapping, which confirms the presence of carbon, and oxygen elements (Fig. 3d and e). Further, FESEM images confirm the uniform distribution of elements on the surface (Fig. 3f). The EDX spectrum of CNDs indicates that oxygen (60.55%) content is higher than that of carbon (39.45%) which is due to the presence of various oxygen-containing functional groups (Figure SI2). It has been already reported that higher oxygen content in CNDs will be effective in protein inhibition which might help for more diagnosis applications [77].

To authorize the size and morphology of the CNDs, HRTEM images were recorded with different magnifications (Fig. 4 a-e). The HRTEM images confirm the size of CNDs was approximately 20–30 nm and mostly in a spherical shape (Fig. 4c inset) [78] with an average interplanar spacing of 0.265 nm (Fig. 4d). In addition, SAED patterns specify that the CNDs are spherical structures with two diffraction rings of (002) and (101) planes which are perfectly coordinated to the 2θ positions (28.4° and 43.3°, respectively) obtained from the XRD pattern (Fig. 4f).

In the past, bare and doped CNDs produced from biomass wastes are employed in a variety of biomedical applications, such as aquatic organism bioindicator [79], cell imaging [80], intracellular detection of Hg (II), and Cr (VI) in HEK-293 cell line [81], bioimaging of Fe3+ in HeLa cells [82], long-term cell tracking and cell therapy monitoring [83], attractive for biorefineries, and to study wound healing kinetics. Furthermore, CND exhibit antitumor activity due to remarkable anti-oxidant potential [84], owing to their wide range of biological properties.

Fig. 5 displays the evaluation of the CNDs cell compatibility against L929 Fibroblast cells. Cell viability was observed for 24 h with different concentrations of CNDs (5, 25, 50, 75, 100 μL) as dose-dependent. The 100 and 75 μL concentrations of CNDs showed less cytotoxicity, i.e. 98% and 80% cell survival, respectively. However, the 5, 25, and 50 μL concentrations of CNDs possess approximately 75% cell viability. This reduced cytotoxicity achieved in less concentration is due to the breakdown of carbon molecules in the CNDs structure [85]. Thus, improved cell viability upon increasing the concentration of CNDs supports positive cell communication and encourages the cell proliferation of L929 cells which make these fluorescent CNDs suitable for both in vitro and in vivo bioimaging application.

Living beings respond to harmful conditions like wounds, infections, toxins, and autoimmune injury by launching an extensive defense mechanism known as inflammation [86]. Through the secretion of inflammatory cytokines cyclooxygenase (COX) and lipoxygenase (LOX), inflammatory mediators myeloperoxidase (MPO), and nitric oxide (NO), macrophages play a role in the start, maintenance, and resolution of inflammatory events [87]. Neutrophil migration and aggregation are significant components of inflammation, and proinflammatory cytokines produced by macrophages can cause endothelial cells to express adhesion proteins and promote leukocyte motility [88]. However, during acute inflammation, neutrophil adherence is inhibited by endogenous reactive NO generated by NO synthases (NOs) [89]. To attain this hypothesis, we have carried out anti-inflammatory studies for CNDs against the aforementioned inflammatory cytokines.

CNDs exhibit anti-inflammatory ability when RAW 264.7 cells were treated with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) to create a common cellular inflammation model [90]. As the main component of the outer membrane of gram-negative bacteria, LPS can trigger strong immune responses in the organism [91]. Therefore, the macrophage inflammation model was established using LPS-induced Raw 264.7 cells. Inflammation is commonly associated with upregulated levels of inflammatory cytokines (COX, LOX) and mediators (MPO) and their expression will increase when inflammation occurs. Thus, the secretion levels of COX, LOX, and MPO were detected to evaluate the inflammation state of macrophages by ELISA assay.

One of the main factors for the emergence and progression of inflammation in chronic diseases is oxidative stress [92]. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) were important in causing severe oxidative damage and contributing to inflammation and bleeding lesions. Evaluating the inflammatory state MPO activity allows the further characterization of the redox state alterations [93]. LPS of the model group caused a significant increase in the activities of MPO (0.026 ± 0.01) for 25 and 50 μL concentrations (Fig. 6 ). At higher concentrations (100 μL), the LPS shows lesser enzyme activity at 0.002 U/mL. The CNDs show effective MPO activity against the LPS control group (0.005 ± 0.001 ± 0.002) (Table SI1) for 25, 50, and 100 μL concentrations due to the suppressed ROS action with increased CNDs dose-dependent formation.

The elevated levels of RAW macrophages with the LPS standard (LOX – 10, 19, and 33%; COX – 6, 13, and 34%) for 25, 50, and 100 μL of samples demonstrate the generation of inflammation (Fig. 7 a and b). However, the expressions of LOX and COX were decreased after CNDs treatment confirming that CNDs can mitigate inflammation by inhibiting the secretion of inflammatory cytokines [94]. Further, the LOX and COX activity is dose-dependent with the inhibition of 9, 14, and 25% for 25, 50, and 100 μL (Fig. 7a; Table SI2) and 10, 16, and 26% for 25, 50, and 100 μL, respectively (Fig. 7b; Table SI3). In particular, the COX cytokines inhibits greater inflammatory cells than LPS at the lower concentrations (25 and 50 μL), but in higher concentration (100 μL) it inhibition rate was decreased to 26% which is lesser than LPS. This result reveals that the intercellular ROS activity in the inflammatory cells can be eliminated by the higher dose of CNDs. Thus, the non-toxic nature of CNDs in the inflammatory cells suppressed the formation of ROS which can be evident from the cell viability assay.

Nitric oxide (NO) is a signaling molecule that is synthesized by NOx in a proinflammatory enzyme [95]. The NO incubation in the RAW cells for LPS stimulation (blank control) produced 10, 23, and 30% of NO inhibition for 25, 50, and 100 μL, respectively, whereas the CNDs intensely inhibited the NO production in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 8 a). The behavior of CNDs at 25, 50 and 100 μL significantly inhibit 13, 31 and 46% of NO which is higher compared with the levels produced after LPS alone (Table SI4). NO is a pro-inflammatory mediator that causes inflammation, and excessive NO production as a result of iNOS activation in activated macrophages has been associated with several illnesses [96]. The results showed that there was no detectable NO production in RAW 264.7 cells control group and also in the cells treated with dose-dependent (25, 50, 100 μL/mL) CNDs. This dose-dependency indicates that the CNDs do stimulate NO production [97]. Fig. 8b displays competently repressed LPS-induced NO release (Table SI5). Thus, CNDs do not exhibit NO scavenging activity at concentrations up to 100 μL/mL.

Protein denaturation is one of the many factors that contribute to inflammation. Therefore, the protein, Bovine serum albumin (BSA), was treated with the standard medication. Therefore, we made a comparative study on the impact of CNDs as an anti-inflammatory agent against BSA and determined the mechanism of suppressing protein denaturation [98]. The protein denaturation results in the generation of autoantigens which in some cases of rheumatic disorders, consequences inflammation [99].

The protein denaturation assay was performed to determine the in vitro anti-inflammatory activity of CNDs as concentration-dependent and their protein-denaturation activity with a rate of inhibition [100]. From the assay, the diclofenac sodium doesn't show any change in protein denaturation [101]. The results revealed that the concentration-dependent inhibition of albumin denaturation and the maximum inhibition of 62% were obtained at 50 μL concentration of CNDs (Fig. 9 ). Further at low concentrations, the percentage of inhibition decreased to 58, 36, and 27% for 25, 12.5, and 6.25 μL, respectively. Moreover, the diclofenac sodium showed antiinflammatory activity at IC50 of 20.048 μL/mL in the CNDs inhibitory assay.

QDs like CdSe, CdTe, CdS, and PbS were also applied in vitro and in vivo optical bioimaging processes with the restrictions such as heavy toxic metals that may cause toxicological and pathological problems for health and the environment [102]. The silicon QDs (SiQDs) can be considered alternatives to heavy metal QDs in bioimaging, but they undergo oxidative biodegradation in biological systems and are marginally toxic [103]. Likewise, the gold and silver nanoclusters were considered to be alternatives, but they suffer from poor water solubility and photostability [104]. Therefore, bioimaging needs better biocompatible, less toxicity, excellent photoluminescence, and high photostability material [105]. These abovementioned unique properties were satisfied by rice husk biomass-derived CNDs due to their various bio-functionalities and physicochemical properties.

Zebrafish are a special model type for biomedical research, including the studies of biological processes and human disorders [106]. This is owing to their fully sequenced genome, 90% of the genes are similar to human genes, simplicity of genetic modification, high fecundity, external fertilization, quick development, thin skin, and nearly transparent embryo [107]. Zebrafish can also be used to research a variety of human metabolic problems [108], human clinical trials [109], and the toxicity of drugs [110].

The bioimaging of zebrafish using fluorescent CNDs was carried out by soaked-in and microinjected into the zebrafish embryo which confirmed CNDs' photostability and biocompatibility [111]. From their fluorescence emission, CNDs were positively identified as being dispersed throughout zebrafish embryos developed at 72 h post-fertilization (hpf) and beyond. Fig. 10 represents the bright, fluorescence, and merged images of control and CNDs on zebrafish embryos. In specific, the zebrafish labeled as control displayed a very low fluorescence signal. Nevertheless, after the incubation with CNDs, the zebrafish showed increased fluorescence at different concentrations of CNDs (5, 50, 75, and 100 μL) in the blue region. The fluorescence intensity increases as the CNDs concentration increases due to the bioavailability of the CND particles spread over the zebrafish embryos. All the concentration emits edible fluorescence at the yolk sac, but for 50 and 100 μL concentrations the fluorescence emits at the eyes. In addition to imaging, the findings showed that CNDs were absorbed, distributed, and migrating in zebrafish without causing any negative effects. The bioimaging outcomes thus support the high biocompatibility and low toxicity of CNDs for zebrafish medication screening.

The rice husk biomass waste-derived CNDs shows an amorphous XRD pattern with two distinguished peaks and divulges the hexagonal graphite structure. The spherical shape with 15–20 nm size distribution of CNDs was confirmed by HRTEM analyses and indicates the lattice fringes value as 0.28 nm at the (101) plane. Further, FESEM images describe the agglomerated spherical morphology with higher oxygen content. The binding energy and bonding of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen-containing functional groups were confirmed by XPS studies. The obtained ID/IG intensity ratio (1.23) from Raman analysis revealed the graphitization of CNDs. The excitation-dependent fluorescence property of CNDs might be due to various sizes of CNDs which was well supported by contour mapping results. The CNDs have excellent cell viability (98%) with less cytotoxicity for higher protein denaturation inhibition (62%) ability. Upon imaging the zebrafish larvae under fluorescence microscopy; a bright blue fluorescence was observed at various concentrations due to the conjugation between CNDs and zebrafish larvae. The non-toxic nature of CNDs assists the anti-inflammatory cytokine response and reduces intracellular ROS production. The CNDs promote an anti-inflammatory effect on LPS-induced Raw 264.7 cells. The CNDs in vitro anti-inflammatory results show 34%, 33%, and 40% of inhibition of cyclooxygenase, lipoxygenase, and NO, respectively, with reduced ROS and scavenging activity. Thus, the outcomes from biological studies of CNDs demonstrate significant correlations between the bioactivity, protein denaturation, and anti-inflammatory properties together with cytotoxicity and dose-dependent ability. Overall, these aforementioned results suggest that CNDs derived from rice husk biomass waste could be an efficient material for multiple biological applications due to their physicochemical properties and surface functionalities. Further studies on CNDs as efficient fluorescent probes and effective biomarkers for bioimaging various diseases and drug penetration in the biological samples for diagnosing and therapeutic applications are currently underway in our laboratory.
